searchTerms: the way of saint james - medieval europe's greatest pilgrimage
shortDescription: Santiago de Compostela, meaning "Saint James of the Field of Stars," has been one of the three most important Christian pilgrimage destinations since the medieval period, alongside Jerusalem and Rome.
name: Santiago de Compostela

primaryLocation.name: The Way of Saint James - Medieval Europe's Greatest Pilgrimage

longDescription: Santiago de Compostela, meaning "Saint James of the Field of Stars," has been one of the three most important Christian pilgrimage destinations since the medieval period, alongside Jerusalem and Rome. The city claims to hold the remains of Saint James the Greater, one of Jesus's twelve apostles and the first to be martyred. Saint James martyred in Jerusalem by Herod Agrippa
description:
Explore temples, mountains, groves, and holy sites that have served as gateways between the human and divine realms
                across cultures and millennia. From ancient stone circles to living pilgrimage destinations, discover the places
                where heaven touches earth.
significance: Santiago de Compostela, meaning "Saint James of the Field of Stars," has been one of the three most important Christian pilgrimage destinations since the medieval period, alongside Jerusalem and Rome.

geography: Often features distinctive natural formations associated with sacred narratives

keyMyths.title: The Discovery of St. James's Relics
keyMyths.description: According to legend, in the 9th century, a hermit named Pelagius (Paio) followed a star to a field (Campus Stellae, hence 'Compostela') where he discovered a tomb containing relics believed to be those of St. James the Apostle. This miraculous discovery led to the establishment of a church and the eventual growth of Santiago de Compostela as a major pilgrimage site.

keyMyths.title: The Translation of St. James's Body
keyMyths.description: The narrative recounts how St. James, after preaching in Spain, returned to Judea and was martyred. His disciples then miraculously transported his body by sea to the coast of Galicia, Spain, where it was eventually buried and later rediscovered.

keyMyths.title: St. James the Moorslayer (Santiago Matamoros)
keyMyths.description: During the Reconquista, St. James was believed to have miraculously intervened in battles against the Moors, appearing as a knight on a white horse to lead Christian armies to victory. This image became a powerful symbol of Christian Spain and its fight against Islamic rule.

keyMyths.title: The Jacobean Holy Year
keyMyths.description: When St. James's feast day (July 25th) falls on a Sunday, it is declared a Holy Year (Año Xacobeo), during which pilgrims receive plenary indulgence for their sins upon completing the pilgrimage. This cyclical event reinforces the spiritual significance of the pilgrimage.
keyMyths.source: Papal Bulls and decrees related to Jacobean Holy Years

keyMyths.title: The Codex Calixtinus and the Pilgrim's Guide
keyMyths.description: The Codex Calixtinus, a 12th-century illuminated manuscript, contains a section known as the Pilgrim's Guide, which offers practical advice and spiritual guidance for pilgrims traveling to Santiago. It also includes accounts of miracles associated with St. James and the Camino de Santiago.

sources.name: Historia Compostellana
sources.description: A 12th-century Latin chronicle documenting the history of the Archdiocese of Santiago de Compostela, providing valuable insights into the development of the pilgrimage and the cult of St. James.
sources.title: Historia Compostellana

sources.name: Codex Calixtinus
sources.description: A 12th-century illuminated manuscript containing liturgical texts, musical pieces, and a pilgrim's guide, offering a comprehensive overview of the pilgrimage to Santiago.
sources.title: Codex Calixtinus

sources.name: Estoria de España (Crónica de España)
sources.description: A 13th-century historical work commissioned by Alfonso X of Castile, which includes accounts of St. James's role in the Reconquista and the development of the cult of Santiago.
sources.title: Estoria de España (Crónica de España)

sources.name: Breviarium Apostolorum
sources.description: A collection of texts relating to the lives and acts of the Apostles, containing accounts of St. James's martyrdom and the translation of his relics to Spain. Various versions exist.
sources.title: Breviarium Apostolorum

sources.name: The Pilgrimage to Santiago de Compostela: A Complete Pilgrim's Guide
sources.description: A modern academic guide by Dee Nolan, offering a comprehensive overview of the history, culture, and practical aspects of the pilgrimage to Santiago de Compostela.
sources.title: The Pilgrimage to Santiago de Compostela: A Complete Pilgrim's Guide
